skill.name: frontend-accessibility
skill.description: Build accessible frontend interfaces with semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard navigation, focus management, form labels, alt text, contrast, and screen reader support.
skill.summary: Improve frontend accessibility with semantic markup, keyboard support, ARIA guidance, and WCAG-oriented review.
skill.author: EIS-ITS

security_audit.summary: The static filesystem finding is a true positive because SKILL.md instructs the agent to follow a parent-directory Markdown link outside the skill package. I found no prompt injection, network access, command execution, or malicious intent in the reviewed file. The weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by normal prose, not cryptographic code.

medium_findings.title: Out-of-Package Relative File Reference
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md tells the agent to refer to a Markdown file through a ../../../ parent-directory path. This can cause the skill to depend on or request access to files outside the packaged skill boundary. The intent appears to be documentation reuse, not malicious access, so this is a moderate packaging and filesystem-boundary concern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory traversal sequence is directly present in a Markdown link. The surrounding text frames it as a documentation reference, which lowers confidence that it is malicious.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Match in Description
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert on SKILL.md line 3 is a substring match in accessibility guidance text. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing operation, or security-sensitive code is present on that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is descriptive metadata about frontend accessibility. I did not find evidence of cryptographic use or weak algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Match in Prose
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert on SKILL.md line 24 is normal explanatory prose about coding standards. No cryptographic primitive or implementation detail appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only describes the purpose of the skill. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code, configuration, or unsafe security advice.

dangerous_patterns.title: Parent Directory Traversal in Documentation Link
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses a ../../../ path in a Markdown link. This is not executable code, but it can encourage file reads outside the skill package when the referenced document is followed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The traversal sequence is explicit and repeated static detections point to the same line. Context shows a documentation link, not an attempt to access secrets.

content.value_statement: Frontend teams often miss keyboard, screen reader, and semantic markup requirements during fast UI work. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for accessible components and WCAG-oriented reviews.
actual_capabilities: Guides semantic HTML structure for templates, JSX, and component markup.
Supports accessible form labels, validation messages, and input relationships.
Prompts keyboard navigation and focus management for interactive elements.
Advises on ARIA attributes for screen reader support when native HTML is not enough.
Encourages review of headings, images, contrast, navigation, dialogs, and dynamic views.
limitations: The skill is guidance-only and does not include automated accessibility test tools.
It references an external standards file through a parent-directory path.
It cannot guarantee legal WCAG compliance without manual and assistive technology testing.
It does not provide framework-specific component libraries or reusable code assets.

use_cases.title: Create Accessible Components
use_cases.description: Use the skill while building buttons, forms, dialogs, menus, and navigation components.

use_cases.title: Review Existing UI Markup
use_cases.description: Ask for accessibility improvements to headings, labels, focus states, image text, and ARIA usage.

use_cases.title: Set Team Accessibility Habits
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent checklist during everyday frontend development and refactoring work.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Simple Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one UI element
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this frontend component for accessibility issues. Focus on semantic HTML, labels, keyboard behavior, and clear fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this form design for accessible labels, validation messages, required fields, error summaries, and screen reader announcements.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Interactive Navigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this navigation or menu interaction for keyboard access, focus order, ARIA roles, escape behavior, and visible focus states.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a WCAG-Oriented Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized accessibility refactor plan for this frontend area. Include risks, manual checks, and implementation steps.

output_examples.input: A login form has placeholder-only labels and error text that appears visually below each field.
output: Add persistent labels connected to each input.
Associate error messages with fields using accessible descriptions.
Provide a clear error summary after submission.
Ensure focus moves to the first invalid field or summary.

output_examples.input: A modal opens from a button and contains several actions.
output: Move focus into the dialog when it opens.
Keep keyboard focus inside the dialog until it closes.
Support Escape to close when appropriate.
Return focus to the trigger after closing.

output_examples.input: A page uses icons, images, nested headings, and low-contrast secondary text.
output: Add useful alt text for informative images.
Hide decorative icons from assistive technology.
Repair heading order so sections are understandable.
Increase text contrast for secondary content.

best_practices: Prefer native HTML controls before adding ARIA.
Test every interactive path with keyboard-only navigation.
Pair automated checks with manual screen reader and focus review.
anti_patterns: Do not replace real labels with placeholder text only.
Do not add ARIA roles that conflict with native element semantics.
Do not hide visible focus indicators without an accessible replacement.

faq.question: Does this skill run an accessibility scanner?
faq.answer: No. It provides coding and review guidance, but it does not run automated tests.

faq.question: Can it help with React or Vue components?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explicitly covers HTML templates, JSX, and component markup.

faq.question: Does it guarantee WCAG compliance?
faq.answer: No. It supports WCAG-oriented work, but compliance needs project-specific testing and review.

faq.question: What accessibility areas does it emphasize?
faq.answer: It emphasizes semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard navigation, focus management, forms, images, headings, and contrast.

faq.question: Is there a security concern?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill references a standards file outside its package using a parent-directory path.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Frontend developers, reviewers, and engineering teams that want consistent accessibility guidance can use it.
